sentences.text: I was standing there with my woolen socks.
I mention the woolen socks only because I-- I strongly believe that they were part of-- great part of saving my life.
The wound that I suffered was a flesh wound.
It didn't shatter any bone.
It didn't sever any major artery.
It did not sever any major nerve, although there was nerve damage.
And because the blood started to flow-- the blood that started to flow was absorbed in the socks.

And because it was cold outside, and because there was nothing to cover the socks-- there were no long pants, the blood coagulated quickly, and essentially formed a pressure bandage over the wound and stopped the flow of blood this way.
And there was no blood flowing.
Obviously, I never removed those socks for the coming couple of days.
I now understand.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] The bullets were not contaminated.
They were heated, so they are sterile, in a sense.
There was a pressure bandage.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] The wound was not contaminated, and I was OK.
I was taken to the mountain, up the mountain, carried by Henry, who promised me that my mother and sister will come later.
Yes.
Yes.
And I knew they were dead.
I don't know how I knew.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't think I knew at that point, but I knew they were dead when I came to the house.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know how long we stayed in the mountain.
It-- it was one or two weeks, but not very long.
The-- the partisan group made a base in the mountain.
They eventually went back down and took everything they could in the way of food and tools and anything which can be salvaged.
Eventually came back down again and see that the Germans gathered the corpses, and so on.
And understood it is now under German control and never to come back.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Eventually [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they decided that I cannot stay with them-- child among the group, in the middle of the winter, in the mountains of-- of Slovakia.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went down from the mountain to a very, very small village called Buly, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so small it's not on a map.
Again, what I told you before that I question my recollections, when I eventually got a map of Slovakia to try and retrace, there was no such place on the map.
There was another, bigger village-- the center, which is also a church, called Donovaly, and I found Donovaly on the map.
We actually have some artifacts from Donovaly, which I brought.
I couldn't find Buly, so I-- but again, Buly was there.
Eventually, I found it.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He brought me to the edge of Buly and placed me with a family-- Christian family there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now there are two versions of what happened when I was placed there-- his version and my recollection.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I have no reason to doubt his version, but maybe it's a bit more dramatized than it was, because my recollection doesn't agree with it totally.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Henry's version is that he brought me to the place and put me in the house of this family-- and I have the names.
I'll describe the house in a moment.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That they frightened them very much by their mere appearance-- armed, unkempt men, with hand grenades hanging from their clothes, with guns.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Not real calmed, I'm sure.
Coming in the middle of the night.
His version is that he threatened them that if they will not keep me well, they will come and kill them and burn the house on them.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I recall from the time, and I recall it very clearly, only a deep care by that family.
Doing things that-- that clearly show that they wanted to save me.
And from what I know now, I believe that my version is the correct one.
Yes, they may have been frightened, without any doubt.
Yes, they may have been threatened.
But I don't think they were totally unwilling participants.
That village has more than me saved, including a neighbor which saved somebody Jewish-- a woman with her child.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is the bright side of-- of the Holocaust, if there's any bright side.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember being placed there.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They gave me food on my arrival.
That, I recall very, very well, because I didn't know what I was eating.
It was very good, and I didn't know at the time what it was, and they never served it again.
And when I asked for it, they told me it was sausages.
And they cannot serve it again, because they have none.
There are no sausages available anymore.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I'm sure they were not kosher, but I don't think it was a concern of mine at the time.
But I felt very much wanted, and I felt very much loved by them.
And I think that what happened later supports and proves this point, and I'll show you some artifacts.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] There were three people in the house.
The house was one big hut.
It was a poor house.
It was a log house.
There was no electricity.
There was no toilet in the house, obviously.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was typical farmer's house, built around the stove.
It was used for heating and for cooking, for sleeping and for everything else.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was a couple.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Young couple, I think.
They had no children at the time.
Their first child was born after I left-- long after I left, after the war-- well, maybe not long, but after I left.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
He was a Pole who married a Slovak, and they lived there with her brother.
Her name-- you want the names?
Sure.
Her name was Pavlina.
His name was Jozef.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Their family name was Striharcek.
And she was obviously called Striharcekova because that the way the Slavic names are inclined with-- for-- for a woman.
The uncle-- I mean the brother's name, was Jan, and his last name was Bura.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they lived there together.
Jozef was a carpenter.
Sorry, not a carpenter, a cobbler.
He-- he made shoes, and I helped him in making shoes.
I had a specific role in making shoes, which I'll describe to you in moment.
But coming back to my perception, why-- why I'm perceiving--- they washed me.
They-- they discovered my wound.
They summoned somebody to-- to deal with the wound.
It was a Jewish doctor, which was hiding in the village, which I eventually met.
And it was Miller.
He was an ophthalmologist in Haifa later on.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And they gave me a name.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They gave me a cover story that they invented on the spot, immediately, the first time.
The named me Jan-- kind of obvious.
There was another Jan there, but they knew  a Jan-- or John-- is a very, very, very standard name in Slovakia, Jan.
And my last name was Suchy, which means, in Slovak means dry.
I finally came out and be dry and-- and warm.
So my name was Jan Suchy.

But what's more important to show how-- how these very primitive people were-- were very intelligent in the sense of life intelligence, life smarts-- they taught me immediately a prayer, a Christian prayer.
They say, we will teach you, and they said the name of the prayer.
And for me, the name of the prayer had no meaning.
They say we will teach you Otche Nash.
It's not a-- it's not a Slovak word, octche nash.
I didn't recognize it.
Only many, many, many years later-- only many years later, when I became interested in religions, I realized that otche nash is not one word.
It's two words, which is Otche Nash, which is Our Father, which is the beginning of the basic Christian our Father in heaven, the sermon of the mount prayer-- the basic prayer of-- of Christianity.
And they taught me that because the Germans would ask children to recite the Otche Nash, and if you didn't know it, you were Jewish, and you were shot.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] If you didn't know it, if you were not Jewish, you were shot anyway, but that's-- that's another story.
Christian children knew our Father in Heaven.
It was taught with them.
Same way the Jewish-- Jewish children were taught [INAUDIBLE] I was-- I-- I recited [NON-ENGLISH] every evening from-- from the time I recollect being alive, not knowing what it means.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So they taught me the Otche Nash.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's interesting that the word was so blurred to each other, that although I spoke perfect-- not perfect, I mean seven-year-old child Slovak, I didn't understand all the words.
But that was the prayer, Funnily enough, for a Jew, not understanding your prayer was very-- very obvious.
I didn't understand the Jewish prayer I was praying.
I knew how to read them, but I didn't understand the words, not all of them.
Part of them, but not all of them.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So I recollect only care and love and worry and support.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They fed me before they ate.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I-- I didn't feel that I were forced on them, and if I was, it may have been the beginning, not toward the end.
And I'll tell you, when we come to the proper time, why.
There, where I met the first Germans.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And there is where I-- where I learned how to distinguish between the types of Germans, between the Wehrmacht and the SS-- in our case, the Waffen-SS-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] between the occasional Gestapo, between the patrols patrolling in between the garrison type of people.
There was not a garrison resident in Buly but there were people who came, stayed for a couple of hours, sat and had coffee, and I played with their weapon.
That where I became interested in weaponry.
There was this old German soldier-- old.
I don't know how old he was.
Relatively to me, he was old.
He looked to me old.
It may have been the second wave of Wehrmacht This was 1945-- '44, '45.
Germany was over.
This was the remnants of the Germans.
He may have been old.
He-- he-- he showed me how a rifle works, and how a Schmeisser-- Schmeisser is the submachine gun that the Germans used-- how a Schmeisser works and how a grenade works, what is a grenade.
Very friendly.
He didn't know I'm Jewish, I'm sure.
And, and the most thing which I learned is how to dread the people who are really the cruelest.
They were not the Germans.
They were the Ukrainians.
There was a Ukrainian contingent fighting with the German Army.
They were under the command of General Vlasov.
We called them the Vlasovtsy, the people of Vlasov.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They were the cruel ones.
They were the ones that-- that killed and maimed, that-- that hit, that-- that were undisciplined.
The Germans, even the SS, was clearly, even for a child in my recollection, were a disciplined army unit, or an SS unit.
They killed, possibly with passion, when they were told to kill.
I don't think they would kill on a whim.
They would execute.
They would burn.
They would-- but they did.
And I'll come to it in a moment.
But the Vlasovsty were the cruel ones.
The Vlasovsty were the people-- I'll-- I'll give you one episode.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This place was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the mountains.
I'll show you pictures.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It was not a road, not a main road.
It was on a road which went from Donovaly to nowhere.
It was not a strategic road.
It didn't lead to any place.
The place had no-- it was in the valley.
It has no strategic value.
There was no garrison there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So over the night, it was under the control of the partisans.
And over the day, they would retreat or-- or walk around until their scouts have seen a German patrol.
They will retreat to the mountains.
It was kind of a semi no man land, going between the hands, terrorized by both the partisans and the Germans.
Robbed by both of them.
And one day, some partisan decided to sleep in the hut that I was in.
It was log hut, and it had a basement where potatoes were kept, and there was a place beneath the-- beneath the roof.
The partisan came and slept beneath the roof-- and it was warm there because the chimney-- and left.
A week later, two weeks, some time later, the Germans came and looked for partisans.
So they searched every single house, one after another.
And they found-- I don't know how you call it.
I know the Hebrew name, [HEBREW].
It's a piece of metal, in which you put cartridges.
In the Mauser type guns that the German used, you would put in a protrusion and push it in, so you could put five cartridges in a gun very quickly, and load the gun very quickly.
And all the ammunition come with these little pieces of metal, the cartridges were pushed.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They found one of these with five cartridges-- the Germans-- in the upper part.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Jozef was taken for interrogation up the mountain, to the other village, which is on top of the mountain.
Which for me was a big mountain, but now being in Slovakia, it was a little hill.
But there's another little village just [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] half a kilometer, or even less, 300 meters away.
These are little villages.
He was taken there for interrogation.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was a road going up there, and there was a house on the top of the mountain.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And everybody was waiting for him.
And suddenly, the man is coming down the mountain, alive.
Well, coming down the mountain-- obviously alive.
And he came back.
These were the Wehrmacht.
And he was interrogated by the Wehrmacht, by the Commander of the patrol of the people who-- and he told him, yes, there were partisans sleeping there at night.
We cannot do anything.
We like you Germans.
We-- we are terrorized by the partisans.
They are stealing from us.
They are undisciplined.
If you stay here, they can't do that.
And they let him go.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A couple of weeks later, the Vlasovsty came and searched, and didn't find anything, because the Germans cleaned everything up.
But they find something somewhere else, didn't like somebody, and killed everybody there.
They didn't interrogate, just killed them.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were executed next to the house where I was.
There is a plaque that I-- it was 10, 15 meters from the house.
They just shot.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Not right.
I don't know what they found, maybe a scarf.
They would find these cartridges, we all will be shot.
That was the difference.
I don't want to justify the Germans, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but my fear in that village was from the SS and from the Vlasovsty.
The Wehrmacht were just patrols.
They were just soldiers.
But there were-- there were other things there.

The-- in-- in-- in one instance, the Germans burned a house on all the inhabitants because partisans fired from the house, killed three-- three doctors, three German doctors which were walking up-- up this little mountain.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was a gun that the-- large gun, that the partisans-- that was left by the Slovak retreating army, by the Slovak in the Slovak uprising, and the partisans used it to shell the Germans.
And it was just standing there, and I remembered.
When I came back, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was no road leading up.
The house was exactly where it was.
There was a plaque on the house where people were executed.
I have the plaque picture, so I remember that correctly, but there was no gun.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I knew exactly where to go.
We-- we drove the car, and we came-- we came straight to the village.
And I identified the house, and I told my wife ahead of time how the windows will look, and they looked the same.
And we discovered this-- this old lady-- 80 years old lady by the name of Maria Baluhova, which recalled the family.
She gave me the names.
She recalled me living there.
And her mother kept another Jewish woman with her child there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was bothered that I remembered everything, but the road was not there.
And I thought that the Slovaks will have the-- the-- the sense of leaving the old gun there.
It was in the middle of a-- of a beautiful meadow, and it was just absolutely the most reasonable place for a memorial.
I thought they would leave it there.
I was coming down the road into this valley, and there was no gun and there was no road up, so I asked.
And-- and-- and it was one of those things, do I recollect correctly?
So I told her, wasn't there a road?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She said, what road?
And I said, oh, my god, to myself.
I said, the road up there.
She said, how do you know?
Yes, but it was long time ago.
For her, it's history.
And I said-- and then I got encouraged.
I said, was not there a gun there?
She said, how do you remember the gun?
They took it so long ago.
Apparently, they took it after the war to use as metal.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So the recollection was correct.
The cruelty, however, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] stayed with me all this time.
The fact that they kept me so lovingly.
Before you--
Eventually--
Yes, yes.
Yes, well, they created a-- a cover story, what we call in-- in-- in the defense establishment.
I was supposedly the son of-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the son of somebody from their-- from the family.
I don't know how they-- maybe they selected the name Suchy to-- to coincide with somebody they know who was killed, and they took me.
It's not an unusual story.
I have blue eyes still.
I had blond hair at the time.
I have straight nose.
I didn't look Jewish at all.
I-- I spoke fluent Slovak, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] so it was not that-- that, you know, that I have Yiddish or something like that, accent.
So I passed as a-- as a child of one of their family who was killed during the war, not in an army, just-- just killed.
He and his wife, and they took me to their house, and I'm part of their family now.
And they treated me as such.
Yes.
I-- I knew that all the time.
I knew that.
I knew that before I came there, that I should not go with children-- with other children.
But that-- that not even-- that I knew before I came, to beware of other Christian children that accidentally will discover I'm Jewish, and they just beat me.
Well, I think that he-- my father was very well known in the community and highly respected.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He met my mother in Banksa Bystrica and she was, from all the description, nice looking, very intelligent, very warm person.
There were two children, took pity on, they just took pity on us, in a sense, that we were Jewish.
She was Jewish.
The Jews helped each other as much as they can.
I think he was in his 20s.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He-- he's in his 70s now.
He [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] also did more than that.
Every Jew that he met-- after he place me, every Jew that he met, he told him that the son of [INAUDIBLE] Frieder, or Rabbi Frieder, is in this village, in this house, and spread the word.
Indeed, my father, who was in jail, got the word in jail that I am alive and that his wife and daughter were killed.
So the-- the word did spread, and without-- without Henry, he would not discover me.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There is an end to that story, before we go to it, the discovery.
He did discover me.
I was taken.
I was brought away.
He died after the war, but he-- he discovered me, and we headed here together, less than a year until he died.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I wrote a letter.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Hardly to say a letter.
I have it.
I have here a copy.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I wrote a letter to that family in 1945, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in Christmas, 1945.
